sections.h:
CONCLUSION ................................................................................................................ 11

INTRODUCTION

There is an inherent complexity when interviewing Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people that are the focus of the Anunga Guidelines. Therefore, an interviewing officer must remain flexible in their approach when conducting interviews. When an officer is considering interviewing an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, they must understand the possible vulnerabilities and the impact those vulnerabilities have on admissibility (voluntariness and fairness).

The information outlined in this package is equally applicable when interviewing witnesses, including children. An interviewee who does not comprehend what is being asked of them or feels they must answer in a particular way to please the interviewer, may give a flawed account. Flawed information could impact the direction of the investigation or ultimately result in a miscarriage of justice.

A vulnerable person for the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone that falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person; and

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or;

• People who have impaired physical functioning;

• People who are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders;

• People who are from non-English speaking background; or

• People that have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

There are many Aboriginal languages and dialects recognised throughout Western Australia, some of which are only spoken by a small group of people. It is imperative to plan your interview to ensure you are making good quality assessments of the interviewee, incident and legal requirements.

Fairness and voluntariness are judicial requirements of any interview. If communication difficulties are apparent in the interview then there is a very real possibility that these requirements have been breached and deem your interview inadmissible.

Witnesses and suspects need to be assessed in accordance with how they present to police;

deal with them as if it was the first time you have met them. Even if they are well known to you, never presume to know their individual circumstances or situation and always ask questions to ascertain their level of vulnerability. If in doubt, treat them as a vulnerable person to ensure fairness.

There will be occasions where a vulnerable person will not be able to be interviewed due to the lack of cognitive understanding of their rights. If the interviewee does not understand their rights, then, in consultation with your supervisor, a decision not to interview may be made. All decisions of this nature must be recorded.

Evaluating the level of vulnerability of the person at first point of contact is particularly relevant for officers working in the Mid-West Gascoyne, Pilbara and Kimberley Police Districts. The percentage of interviewees suffering from a vulnerability is significantly higher in these districts. Police must consider the varied vulnerabilities that may be present and act accordingly to ensure judicial requirements are met.

In every instance interviewing officers will plan for the interview they are going to conduct in accordance with the Suspect and Witness Investigative Interviewing manuals. These can be located on the Investigative Standards website.

All suspect interviews must be conducted utilising the Investigative Interviewing, Conversation Management model.

Interviewers must endeavour to use open questions. In order to achieve this TEDS and 5W&H type questions should be used. Interviewers should only ask one question at a time and use simple language.

sections.h:
On 30 April 1979, Mr. Justice FORSTER handed down his reasons for rejecting typewritten records of conversations in the matter of QUEEN v Angus ANUNGA, Sandy AJAX, Clancy AJAX, TJINGUNYA, Nari WHEELER and Frankie Miller JAGAMARA in the Northern Territory Supreme Court in August 1975. (R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412) Inter alia Mr. Justice FORSTER said:

"It seems to me now, however, to be important that the Court should put on record general guidelines for the conduct of Police Officers when interrogating Aboriginal persons.

I preface this statement of guidelines by pointing out that Aboriginal people often do not understand English very well and that, even if they do understand the words, they may not understand the concepts which English phrases and sentences express.

Even with the use of interpreters this problem is by no means solved. Police and legal English sometimes is not translatable into the Aboriginal language at all and there are no separate Aboriginal words for some simple words like "in", "at", "on", "by", "with", or "over'', these being suffixes added to the word they qualify. Some words may translate literally into Aboriginal language but mean something different. "Did you go into his house?" means to an English-speaking person, "did you go into the building?", but to an Aboriginal it may also mean, "did you go within the fence surrounding the house?" English concepts of time, number and distance are imperfectly understood if at all by Aboriginal people, many of the more primitive of whom cannot tell the time by a clock. One frequently hears the answer "long time", which depending on the context may be minutes, hours, days, weeks, or years. In case I may be misunderstood, I should also emphasise that I am not expressing the view that Aboriginal people are any less intelligent than white people but simply that their concepts of certain things and the terms in which they are expressed may be wholly different to those of white people.

Another matter which needs to be understood is that most Aboriginal people are basically courteous and polite and will answer questions by white people in the way in which they think the questioner wants. Even if they are not courteous and polite there is the same reaction when they are dealing with an authority figure such as a policeman. Indeed their action is probably a combination of natural politeness and their attitude to someone in authority. Some Aboriginal people find the standard caution quite bewildering, even if they understand that they do not have to answer questions, because if they do not have to answer questions, then why are the questions being asked?

Guidelines to be Followed when Interviewing Aboriginals Bearing in mind these preliminary observations which are based partly upon my own knowledge and partly by evidence I have heard in numerous cases I lay down the following guidelines. They apply, of course, to persons who are being questioned as suspects.1

Based on the above recommendations by Mr. Justice FORSTER, the Anunga rules were formulated as interviewing considerations for Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

It is important to understand that not all Aboriginals or Torres Strait Islander descent people are vulnerable, however interviewing officers must always consider the Anunga Guidelines.

Sound judgement should be applied when applying the Anunga Guidelines, for instance; it may not be necessary to provide the interviewee every Anunga rule on every occasion. A scaled approach reduces the likelihood of appearing condescending and thus increases rapport.

Remember, Anunga Guidelines are about fairness and voluntariness. It is your responsibility to ensure that their application achieves those two requirements.

There are nine Anunga rules (in Italics), each followed with some explanatory notes.

1 R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412

Unless fluent in English provide an interpreter

1. When an Aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as fluent in

English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the Aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding.

If the suspect is communicating with a basic level of understanding of the English language, it is likely they may not fully comprehend instructions or questions asked of them. In all instances, you must use the services of a qualified interpreter. Further, it may become apparent the suspect’s cognitive understanding is still questionable, despite having the assistance of an interpreter. In this instance; consult with your supervisor and decide whether to continue with the interview.

During the interview speak directly to the suspect or witness using short simple phrases.

This improves the interpreter’s ability to interpret the exact words.

Give the option of an 'Interview Friend'

2. When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that a

"prisoner's friend"2 (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend', should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend', be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported.

An interview friend is a person who is present at the request of the suspect. They must be someone known to the suspect, independent of the police, able to speak the suspect's principal language and be someone the interviewee has confidence in. The interview friend must also be impartial to the matter under investigation.

If you cannot locate an interview friend, you should consider using the services of an independent person or decide not to proceed with the interview. Even though there is no statutory obligation to provide a witness with an interview friend, an interview friend may be present for the purposes of assisting in the interview process.

Ensure Caution is given and all elements are

understood

3. Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate to do so.

It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?" Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell 2 Western Australia Police Force use the term Interview Friend them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend" or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it.

The caution may need to be broken down into manageable sections. The police caution consists of three key parts. It is the interviewing officer’s responsibility to ensure the suspect’s explanation of all parts demonstrates their full understanding of the caution.

A simple yes or no answer from the suspect does not demonstrate their understanding If, after this process, you do not feel the suspect understands the caution you should stop the interview and seek advice from a supervisor.

Formulation of questions

4. Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible the answer

which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way. Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question, which may suggest the answer, but also the manner and tone of voice which are used.

Officers should use TEDS (Tell, Explain, Describe, Show) and 5W&H (Who, What, When, Where, Why and How) style questions to obtain open responses from the suspect. Take time to formulate questions during the interview and don’t rush. Keep your questions short and simple, be patient and allow sufficient time to answer. If the suspect does not understand your question, re-phrase it.

Corroborate any admissions from other sources

5. Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating to the

commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources. Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank JAGAMARA.

Always seek to clarify any admissions made by gathering further evidence to support the information given by the suspect. Due to their culture, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander suspects may want to please the interviewer and inadvertently admit to something they have little or no knowledge of.

Offer meals, drinks, have toilets available to use

6. Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of white

authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station, or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water. They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest.

A meal, drink and an offer to use the toilet should be offered to the suspect. The offer of a meal and drink is not necessarily about sustenance but about making the interviewee feel comfortable. As stated earlier, the Anunga Guidelines are applied in a scaled approach, for example, if an interviewee has only just had a meal it may not be necessary to offer one, however that should be addressed during the introduction phase of the interview.

Should not interview anyone who is ill, intoxicated or

tired

7. It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated when

they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time.

If a suspect indicates or you believe they are tired, then the interview will be delayed until they are rested. If the suspect says they are tired and wishes to rest you must facilitate this.

If a suspect presents or indicates as being intoxicated, drug affected or ill you will not interview them. You will allow them to rest/sleep and only interview them once they are in a fit state. Section 141(k) Criminal Investigation Act gives the power to detain a suspect for this purpose. If the interview is lengthy, the suspect requests it or you believe the suspect would benefit from a break, then a break should be considered.

Legal assistance is offered and reasonable steps taken

to obtain it

8. Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain

such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue.

If a suspect requests to speak with a legal practitioner, this is to be allowed either in person or on the telephone. This right is usually exercised before the interview, however, the right may be exercised during or after the interview. Talking with a legal practitioner is an ongoing right of a suspect and if during the interview they request to speak with a legal practitioner you must stop the interview and facilitate the request.

If the suspect indicates they do not want to answer any questions the interview should be stopped immediately, however, the interviewer needs to be mindful of whether the interviewee is merely uncomfortable with that particular topic or whether they are declining to answer any questions.

If clothing is seized/removed, provide substitute

clothing

9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes of

medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

Be mindful that wearing the standard forensic overalls police provide to suspects when there clothing has been seized may be perceived as prejudicial to the suspect if your AVROI is viewed by a magistrate, jury or judge. If practicable, provide them with everyday replacement clothing.

sections.h:
FASD3 is a mental impairment and will effect individuals differently depending on the severity of the condition. The neurological effects of FASD can have some cognitive and behavioral consequences such as; learning disabilities, poor memory, hyperactivity and in some cases, low IQ. These neurological effects may increase the likelihood of suggestibility in individuals with FASD; a term referring to the ability for an individual to be easily persuaded to take another’s position.

This increased vulnerability of suggestibility for individuals with FASD requires careful consideration when interviewing a suspect with FASD, to ensure the interview is conducted with the principles of fairness and voluntariness. In addition to applying the tips outlined below, all suspect interviews will be conducted using the Investigative Interviewing and Conversation Management model.

Individuals with suspected or confirmed FASD, function highest in structured and predictable environments. At the beginning of the interview, it may be helpful to explain the format of the interview and the type of questions that will be asked in detail, to decrease the likelihood of adverse emotional and behavioural reactions. In addition to this, individuals with FASD may have sensory processing disturbances that make crowds, noise, or bright lights difficult to manage. These conditions can cause agitation and difficulty to focus. Therefore, it may be helpful to conduct the interview in a quiet and softly lit environment with limited persons present in the room.

An adult with FASD frequently presents with the same development and functioning capabilities of half their chronological age (dysmaturity). This factor should be taken into account and reflected in the simple language used by the interviewer. This is especially important when ascertaining if the individual can display a level of understanding of their legislative rights and the caution.

In accordance with the Investigative Interviewing and Conversational Management model, interviewers should provide the individual with an opportunity to tell their version of the story, uninterrupted at the outset of the interview session. Interviewers should rely on open questions (TEDS, 5W&H) and check often for a level of understanding. Interviewers should also only ask one question at a time and use simple language, as those with FASD have 3 Mental Health Act 2014 s 6(1)

significant difficulty understanding abstract concepts and making inferences from context.

Multiple questions asked in the one sentence should be avoided.

Interviewers should refrain from using judgmental or accusatory tones or phrases because this can heighten anxiety and limit information processing in individuals with FASD. Similarly, using overly complimentary language may encourage the individual to falsely answer questions, in order to please authority. In all instances, seek corroborative evidence to confirm the suspect’s version of events.

If individuals with FASD are struggling to give verbal responses, allow them to communicate in a different way. If appropriate, consider allowing them to act it out or draw pictures to assist their response. Alternatively, consider writing out the questions and allow them to write out their response.

Suggestibility coupled with memory deficiencies may lead to confabulation. Confabulation is an unintentional effort to fill in gaps of memory, without the intention to be untruthful or mislead others4. Suggestion and confabulation are particularly likely in situations where interviewers ask leading questions or pressure interviewees. Interviewers should only challenge a suspect’s version of events in the ‘Challenge Phase’ of an interview, in line with the Conversation Management model.

If reading is required in the interview, allow adequate reading time for individuals with FASD and offer the option of reading the material aloud. Be sure to verify comprehension as some individuals with FASD develop strategies such as displaying strong emotional reactions, to avoid detection of their poor reading skills, even if they agreed to read the material.

Individuals with FASD generally work harder to focus on social interactions, so they may only be able to handle a few minutes of an interview at a time. If pushed too hard, they may become withdrawn (shut down or not speak at all) or volatile (overly emotional, crying, screaming or violent towards self and others). In this emotional state, there is risk the individual may provide false or inaccurate information as a strategy to cope with the inability to handle the anxious situation. In line with Investigative Interviewing principles, taking breaks in the case of interviewing someone with FASD, may increase the likelihood of gaining more accurate information. A break could include suspending the interview and allowing the individual to leave the room for a period of time, if practicable.

Many people with FASD find their brains work better when their bodies are engaged in some form of movement. It may be helpful if the individual engages in some form of physical activity such as; pacing about the room, doodling, fidgeting with a stress ball or other small benign item during the interview, if safe to do so.

FASD and suggestibility may increase the likelihood of false confessions during police interviews. This increases the risk of interviews deemed inadmissible if vulnerabilities are not adequately identified and addressed. By following these tips outlined above and increasing awareness about FASD, these risks may be decreased.5 4 Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder and Suggestibility/Brown et al. British Journal of Clinical Psychology (1993) 5 Foetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder and Suggestibility/Brown et al. British Journal of Clinical Psychology (1993)

CONCLUSION

All interviews will be conducted using the Investigative Interviewing Model which is the Western Australia Police Force standard. This document includes information to assist officers when interviewing people with vulnerabilities, specifically Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander people in the Mid-West Gascoyne, Kimberley and Pilbara districts. Any officer who fails to adhere to the guidance in this document risks their interview or statement being ruled inadmissible, miscarriages of justice, poor service delivery to the community and substantial embarrassment to the Western Australia Police Force.

Further information can be located on the Investigative Interviewing page on the Investigative Standards website.
sections.b: FASD3 is a mental impairment and will effect individuals differently depending on the severity of the condition. The neurological effects of FASD can have some cognitive and behavioral consequences such as; learning disabilities, poor memory, hyperactivity and in some cases, low IQ. These neurological effects may increase the likelihood of suggestibility in individuals with FASD; a term referring to the ability for an individual to be easily persuaded to take another’s position. This increased vulnerability of suggestibility for individuals with FASD requires careful consideration when interviewing a suspect with FASD, to ensure the interview is conducted with the principles of fairness and voluntariness. In addition to applying the tips outlined below, all suspect interviews will be conducted using the Investigative Interviewing and Conversation Management model. Individuals with suspected or confirmed FASD, function highest in structured and predictable environments. At the beginning of the interview, it may be helpful to explain the format of the interview and the type of questions that will be asked in detail, to decrease the likelihood of adverse emotional and behavioural reactions. In addition to this, individuals with FASD may have sensory processing disturbances that make crowds, noise, or bright lights difficult to manage. These conditions can cause agitation and difficulty to focus. Therefore, it may be helpful to conduct the interview in a quiet and softly lit environment with limited persons present in the room. An adult with FASD frequently presents with the same development and functioning capabilities of half their chronological age (dysmaturity). This factor should be taken into account and reflected in the simple language used by the interviewer. This is especially important when ascertaining if the individual can display a level of understanding of their legislative rights and the caution. In accordance with the Investigative Interviewing and Conversational Management model, interviewers should provide the individual with an opportunity to tell their version of the story, uninterrupted at the outset of the interview session. Interviewers should rely on open questions (TEDS, 5W&H) and check often for a level of understanding. Multiple questions asked in the one sentence should be avoided. Interviewers should refrain from using judgmental or accusatory tones or phrases because this can heighten anxiety and limit information processing in individuals with FASD. Similarly, using overly complimentary language may encourage the individual to falsely answer questions, in order to please authority. In all instances, seek corroborative evidence to confirm the suspect’s version of events. Individuals with FASD generally work harder to focus on social interactions, so they may only be able to handle a few minutes of an interview at a time. If pushed too hard, they may become withdrawn (shut down or not speak at all) or volatile (overly emotional, crying, screaming or violent towards self and others). A break could include suspending the interview and allowing the individual to leave the room for a period of time, if practicable.
